expected_behavior: Rejects prompt-language-alone as the defense: requires structurally fencing the untrusted document text in explicit delimiters (e.g. XML-style tags) that the system prompt names, with trusted instructions and attacker-influenceable content in separate zones — never bare f-string concatenation of doc_text into the instruction stream

Raises delimiter forgery: a document containing a planted closing delimiter can break out of the fence, so delimiter look-alikes in the untrusted text must be neutralized/defanged (tolerant of whitespace/case variants) while remaining visible
Requires an embedded directive to be surfaced as a finding/signal about the document (evidence of an injection attempt), not just silently ignored

Requires validating the model's output before it is persisted or acted on — schema/shape validation plus allowlisting actionable content (URLs, recipients, paths, commands) so an injected payload can't ride out in a valid-shaped result
Turns last week's incident into a durable regression guard: an injection test corpus (including the exact bypass) wired into CI/evals asserting the verdict doesn't flip and the schema stays intact
anti_behavior: Approves the IMPORTANT-line fix as sufficient
Proposes only strengthening or repeating the natural-language warning without structural delimiting
Ignores the output-validation half (treats input-side fencing as the complete defense)
Omits any regression test/eval for the known bypass
source: references/secure-data-processing.md §2 worked example (two-zone fence, delimiter neutralization, injection-as-finding) — B2 coverage slice, v1.14
